coredata.dc:title: Posidonia oceanica (Mediterranean tapeweed) leaf litter as a source of fluorescent carbon dot preparations
coredata.dc:creator: Christopoulou, Natalia-Maria
Kalogianni, Despina P.
Christopoulos, Theodore K.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) represent a new and highly promising class of nanomaterials with a plethora of applications as novel analytical tools. Their physicochemical properties are determined by the nature of the precursors and the conditions used for their preparation. Nowadays, there is an increasing interest on the use of naturally occurring substances and especially renewable material for their synthesis. In this context, we report the hydrothermal synthesis of aqueous fluorescent carbon dot preparations from Posidonia oceanica (Mediterranean tapeweed) leaf litter, thus transforming waste material to valuable analytical tools. The prepared CDs were nearly spherical (size 1–5 nm) and highly soluble in water due to the presence of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on their surface. They emitted excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence. The effect of pH and ionic strength were studied as well as the influence of various metal ions. Carbon dots derived from P. oceanica leaf litter in water, in aqueous ethylenediamine solution and in aqueous cysteamine solution gave fluorescence quantum yields of 3.8%, 11% and 10.6%, respectively. Furthermore, we demonstrated that carbon dot preparations derived from P. oceanica leaf litter can be used as analytical reagents for the fluorometric determination of Fe3+, Cr2O7 2- and CrO4 2-.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Mediterranean tapeweed
Posidonia oceanica
Leaf litter, Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Carbon dot preparations from P. Oceanica
Absorption, fluorescence and X-Ray photoelectron spectra
Effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence
Effect of pH on the fluorescence
Effect of various ions on the fluorescence
Fluorometric method for determination of Fe3+, CrO4 2- and Cr2O7 2-

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Department of Chemistry, University of Patras

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Institute of Chemical and Engineering Sciences

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge support of this work by the project ?Research Infrastructure on Food Bioprocessing Development and Innovation Exploitation ? Food Innovation RI? (MIS 5027222), which is implemented under the Action ?Reinforcement of the Research and Innovation Infrastructure?, funded by the Operational Programme ?Competitiveness, Entrepreneurship and Innovation? (NSRF 2014-2020) and co-financed by Greece and the European Union (European Regional Development Fund). The authors thank Dr. Mary Kollia from the Laboratory of Electron Microscopy and Microanalysis of the University of Patras for her help with the acquisition of TEM images. We are grateful to Prof. Nikolaos Vouroutzis from the Department of Solid State Physics of the Aristotelian University of Thessaloniki for obtaining the HRTEM images. We also thank Dr. Amaia Soto Biobide from the Laboratory of Applied Molecular Spectroscopy of the Institute of Chemical Engineering Sciences (ICE-HT/FORTH) for her help in obtaining the FT-IR spectra. We would like to thank Dr. Labrini Sygellou from ICE-HT/FORTH for the XPS measurements and analysis. We are also thankful to Prof. Spyridon Perlepes from the Department of Chemistry, University of Patras, for his valuable comments on ATR spectra.

We acknowledge support of this work by the project “Research Infrastructure on Food Bioprocessing Development and Innovation Exploitation – Food Innovation RI” (MIS 5027222), which is implemented under the Action “Reinforcement of the Research and Innovation Infrastructure”, funded by the Operational Programme “Competitiveness, Entrepreneurship and Innovation” (NSRF 2014-2020) and co-financed by Greece and the European Union (European Regional Development Fund). The authors thank Dr. Mary Kollia from the Laboratory of Electron Microscopy and Microanalysis of the University of Patras for her help with the acquisition of TEM images. We are grateful to Prof. Nikolaos Vouroutzis from the Department of Solid State Physics of the Aristotelian University of Thessaloniki for obtaining the HRTEM images. We also thank Dr. Amaia Soto Biobide from the Laboratory of Applied Molecular Spectroscopy of the Institute of Chemical Engineering Sciences (ICE-HT/FORTH) for her help in obtaining the FT-IR spectra. We would like to thank Dr. Labrini Sygellou from ICE-HT/FORTH for the XPS measurements and analysis. We are also thankful to Prof. Spyridon Perlepes from the Department of Chemistry, University of Patras, for his valuable comments on ATR spectra.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: P. oceanica leaf litter and carbon dot preparations. (A) Dead leaves, (B) Carbon dot solution (in natural light) after preparation and filtration, (C) Carbon dot solution (in natural light) in a quartz cuvette after dilution. (D) As in (C) but irradiated with UV light at 365 nm. (E) Fluorescence images of POS preparations within the quartz cuvette at λex = 340 nm (left) and λex = 380 nm (right).

FT-IR spectra of carbon dots derived through hydrothermal treatment of P. oceanica leaf litter in water (POS), in aqueous solution of ethylenediamine (POS-ETH) and in aqueous solution of cysteamine (POS-CYSTE).

Fluorescence emission spectra of carbon dot preparations derived through hydrothermal treatment of P. oceanica leaf litter in water (POS), in aqueous solution of ethylenediamine (POS-ETH) and in aqueous solution of cysteamine (POS-CYSTE).

Effect of pH on the fluorescence emission spectra of carbon dots derived by hydrothermal treatment of P. oceanica leaf litter in water (POS), in aqueous solution of ethylenediamine (POS-ETH) and in aqueous solution of cysteamine (POS-CYSTE).

Fluorometric determination of Fe3+ by using P. oceanica carbon dot preparation as an analytical reagent. Left panel: Fluorescence emission spectra of the carbon dot preparation in the presence of various Fe3+ concentrations. Right panel: Plot of the F0/F ratio vesrsus the final Fe3+ concentration in the sample. F0 is the fluorescence intensity at zero Fe3+ concentration and F is the fluorescence intensity of the samples.

Fluorometric determination of Cr2O7 2- by using P. oceanica carbon dot preparation as an analytical reagent. Left panel: Fluorescence emission spectra of the carbon dot preparation in the presence of various Cr2O7 2- concentrations. Right panel: Plot of the F0/F ratio vesrsus the final Cr2O7 2- concentration in the sample. F0 is the fluorescence intensity at zero Cr2O7 2- concentration and F is the fluorescence intensity of the samples.
Percent (%) elemental composition of POS, POS-ETH, POS-CYSTE, ethylenediamine (ETH) and cysteamine (CYSTE) samples (experimental error: ±0.1).
Percent (%) carbon components composition of POS, POS-ETH, POS-CYSTE, ethylenediamine (ETH) and cysteamine (CYSTE) samples (experimental error: ±0.5).

Quantum yields obtained from various preparations of carbon dots from P. oceanica. In each case, 2 g of P. oceanica dead leaves were added to 20 mL of water or aqueous ethylenediamine or cysteamine solutions.

head.ce:title: Posidonia oceanica (Mediterranean tapeweed) leaf litter as a source of fluorescent carbon dot preparations

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Patras, Patras 26504, Greece
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of Patras, Patras 26504, Greece

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemical Engineering Sciences, Foundation for Research and Technology Hellas (FORTH/ICE), Patras 26504, Greece

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Foundation for Research and Technology Hellas (FORTH/ICE)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Chemical Engineering Sciences, Foundation for Research and Technology Hellas (FORTH/ICE), Patras 26504, Greece

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, University of Patras, Patras 26504, Greece.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) represent a new and highly promising class of nanomaterials with a plethora of applications as novel analytical tools. Their physicochemical properties are determined by the nature of the precursors and the conditions used for their preparation. Nowadays, there is an increasing interest on the use of naturally occurring substances and especially renewable material for their synthesis. In this context, we report the hydrothermal synthesis of aqueous fluorescent carbon dot preparations from Posidonia oceanica (Mediterranean tapeweed) leaf litter, thus transforming waste material to valuable analytical tools. The prepared CDs were nearly spherical (size 1–5 nm) and highly soluble in water due to the presence of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on their surface. They emitted excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence. The effect of pH and ionic strength were studied as well as the influence of various metal ions. Carbon dots derived from P. oceanica leaf litter in water, in aqueous ethylenediamine solution and in aqueous cysteamine solution gave fluorescence quantum yields of 3.8%, 11% and 10.6%, respectively. Furthermore, we demonstrated that carbon dot preparations derived from P. oceanica leaf litter can be used as analytical reagents for the fluorometric determination of Fe3+, Cr2O7 2- and CrO4 2-.

ce:section.ce:para: Posidonia oceanica (Mediterranean tapeweed, Neptune seagrass) is a marine plant found only in the Mediterranean basin. It forms large meadows in sandy areas covering a total area of ​​40000 km2 and at depths of 1–50 m [1]. It belongs to the angiosperms (monocotyledons) and carries roots, stems, leaves, flowers and fruits. The leaves are green, up to 1 m long and up to 1 cm wide. P. oceanica plays a very important role in the marine ecosystem of the Mediterranean basin because: (a) It captures huge amounts of CO2 and releases O2 into the marine environment. (b) With its root system it stabilizes the seafloor. (c) It is a source of food and a shelter for a variety of marine organisms. The leaves of P. oceanica are constantly renewed. The plant throws its leaves during the autumn. Leaf production is estimated to be between 300 and 2000 g m−2 year−1. As a result, huge amounts of dead P. oceanica leaves accumulate on the coast and their removal is associated with a high cost. Their decomposition releases H2S. Usually, this biomass is collected and ends up in landfills [2]. P. oceanica leaves contain high levels of cellulose, semi-cellulose, and lignin. P. oceanica lignin is rich in p-hydroxybenzoic acid that is bound to lignin by esterification with 2-methoxyphenol and 2,6-dimethoxyphenol groups [3,4]. It also contains phenolic compounds, phenylmethane derivatives, phenylpropanoic acid, etc. [5,6]. Due to the problems caused by the accumulation of dead leaves of P. oceanica on the coast (especially during the tourist season), various ways of utilizing this biomass have been proposed. Suggested uses for leaf litter are: (i) Removal of pollutants from aqueous solutions by sorption processes. In particular, fibers and particles derived from P. oceanica leaves have been used to remove pigments found in textile industry waste [7–10]. (ii) As a source of cellulose for paper making and other uses [11–12]. (iii) As a fertilizer and material for composting [13]. (iv) As a biofuel [13].

The aim of the present work is to convert dead leaves of P. oceanica seagrass, into fluorescent preparations of carbon dots, which constitute highly promising nanomaterials with a plethora of applications as novel analytical tools. Carbon dots represent a new class of carbon nanoparticles [14,15]. They are nearly spherical with a typical size of less than 10 nm. CDs vary from highly amorphous to highly graphitic in nature and their surface consists of various functional groups. Fluorescence is perhaps the most interesting property of carbon dots as far as analytical method development is concerned. CDs have already been used for the fluorometric determination of metal ions [16,17], anions [18], thiols [19], glucose [20], NO2 [21], drugs [22], vitamins [23], pesticides [24], nitroaromatic explosives [25], proteins [26], enzymes [27], DNA [28], cells [29] and bacteria [30], as well as in vitro and in vivo imaging [31–38]. Their key advantages include excellent water dispersibility, low toxicity compared to semiconductor (e.g. CdSe) quantum dots, better biocompatibility and rapid renal clearance.

CDs are synthesized either from large carbon particles by decomposition (top- down approaches) or from small organic molecules via a polymerization, dehydration, carbonization process (bottom-up methods). The physicochemical properties of the CDs are determined by the nature of the precursors, as well as the conditions used for their preparation. Because of the widespread and increasing interest on CDs, considerable efforts have been put on their facile synthesis from naturally occurring substances and especially from renewable material, such as coffee grounds [39], pomelo peel [40], milk [41], tomato juice [42], apple juice [43], chicken egg [44], human hair fibers [45], human urine [46], banana [32], gooseberry [35], magnolia [36] and peach [38]. To this end, we demonstrate the synthesis, the characteristics and the analytical performance of fluorescent carbon dot preparations using P. oceanica leaf litter as a source.

Fluorescence spectroscopy was performed with the Perkin-Elmer LS-50B (Perkin-Elmer, UK) and the Shimadzu RF-1501 (Shimadzu, Japan) spectrofluorometers. UV–Vis absorption spectra were obtained using the Nanophotometer (Implen, Germany). Infrared absorption spectra (FT-IR) were acquired by using the Bruker Equinox 55 (Bruker, Billerica MA) equipped with the attenuated total reflectance (ATR) accessory. XPS measurements were performed in a UHV chamber (P ~ 5 × 10−10 mbar) equipped with a SPECS Phoibos 100-1D-DLD hemispherical electron analyzer and a non-monochromatized dual-anode Mg/Al X-ray source for XPS. The XP Spectra were recorded with MgKa at 1253.6 eV photon energy and an analyzer pass energy of 10 eV giving a Full Width at Half Maximum (FWHM) of 0.85 eV for Ag3d5/2 line. The analyzed area was a spot of 3 mm diameter. The atomic ratios were calculated from the intensity (peak area) of the XPS peaks weighted with the corresponding relative sensitivity factors (RSF) derived from the Scofield cross-section and the energy analyzer transmission function. For spectra collection and treatment, including fitting, the commercial software SpecsLab Prodigy (by Specs GmbH, Berlin) was used.The XPS peaks were fitted by decomposing each spectrum into individual mixed Gaussian-Lorentzian peaks after a Shirley background subtraction. The samples were in aqueous solution and drop casted on ITO coated glass.
Dead leaves from P. oceanica seaweed were obtained from the coast and stored at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dot preparations from P. Oceanica

ce:section.ce:para: Typically, an amount of 2 g of P. oceanica dead leaves was added in 20 mL water and placed in a 30-mL teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The autoclave was maintained at 180 °C for 6 h, and was then left to cool down slowly to room temperature. The resulting solution was first filtered through a plain filter paper, then filtered again through a 0.2-um hydrophilic filter (Millipore, MA, USA) and finally centrifuged at 15,000 g for 30 min. The supernatant was then subjected to lyophilization.

For the preparation of P. oceanica CDs in aqueous ethylenediamine solutions (POS-ETH), 2 g of P. oceanica dead leaves were added to 20 mL of a 2.25 mmol/L ethylenediamine solution in water and the procedure was the same as above except that the hydrothermal conditions were set at 200 °C for 8 h.

For the preparation of P. oceanica CDs in aqueous cysteamine solutions (POS-CYSTE), 2 g of P. oceanica dead leaves were added to 20 mL of a 9.3 mmol/L cysteamine solution in water and the procedure was the same as above except that the temperature was 200 °C for 8 h.

The relative quantum yields of the CDs were determined by using quinine sulphate (1 umol/L in 0.05 mol/L H2SO4) as a reference fluorophore. The same excitation wavelength, slit band width and gain were used for the CDs and the quinine sulphate solutions. The calculations were performed according to the following equation:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorption, fluorescence and X-Ray photoelectron spectra
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows typical images of the P. oceanica dead leaves used as a source for the synthesis of carbon dots. The as-prepared CDs have excellent water solubility. Their aqueous suspensions are transparent and have orange to dark brown color at ambient light. They exhibit blue fluorescence when irradiated with a 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 1). The red shift of fluorescence emission of carbon dots with increasing excitation wavelength is also shown in Fig. 1. The carbon dots solution emits blue fluorescence when excited at 340 nm (left) whereas excitation at 380 nm leads to a higher emission wavelength (right).

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs prepared in water (POS), in ethylenediamine (POS-ETH), and in cysteamine (POS-CYSTE) are presented in the Electronic Supplementary Material (ESM) Figure S1. In the absorption spectrum of POS-H2O one peak is observed at 259 nm and another at 316 nm. In the presence of ethylenediamine (POS-ETH) two peaks, at 283 nm and 329 nm, are observed. In the presence of cysteamine two peaks are also observed at 274 nm and 317 nm. The peaks of the absorption spectrum are attributed to π → π* transitions of the C = C bonds and the n → π* transition of C = O.

The functional groups of carbon nanoparticle preparations were characterized by infrared spectrometry. The FT-IR spectra were obtained immediately after lyophilization. FT-IR spectra of P. oceanica CD preparations derived from water (POS), aqueous ethylenediamine (POS-ETH) and aqueous cysteamine solutions (POS-CYSTE) are presented in Fig. 2 . CDs exhibit a wide absorption band from about 3500 to 3000 cm−1 (3277 cm−1 for POS, 3225 cm−1 from POS-ETH and 3126 cm−1 from POS-CYSTE) corresponding to stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H. Also, the peaks corresponding to about 2932 cm−1 (POS), 2972 ​​cm−1 (POS-ETH) and 3038 cm−1 (POS-CYSTE) are attributed to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of the C–H bonds. The peaks at approximately 1586 cm−1 (POS), 1579 cm−1 (POS-ETH) and approximately 1593 cm−1 (POS-CYSTE) are attributed to vibrations of the C = C and N–H bonds (for POS-ETH and POS-CYSTE). Also, the peaks from about 1419 to about 1340 cm−1 are attributed to the C-O, C-N = vibrations (for POS-ETH and POS-CYSTE) and to carboxyl group vibrations. In addition, the peaks at about 1087 and 1047 cm−1 are attributed to the absorption of the C-O bonds, and C-O-C bonds. Finally, the peak at 1106 cm−1 of POS-ETH is attributed to vibrations of the C-O and C-N bonds. The peak at about 2813 cm−1 of POS-CYSTE corresponds to S-H vibrations. From these data, it is deduced that the surface of the CDs contains hydroxyl groups, carboxyl groups and/or epoxy groups which impart excellent water solubility. [51,52].

The fluorescence emission spectra of P. oceanica carbon dots preparations in water (POS), and in aqueous solutions of ethylenediamine (POS-ETH) and cysteamine (POS-CYSTE), respectively, are presented in Fig. 3 . The emission spectra have been obtained at various excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 nm to 480 nm. It is observed that the wavelength of maximum emission (λemmax) depends on the excitation wavelength and increases with the excitation wavelength. This is a frequently encountered property of carbon dots [7]. Although the mechanism of the fluorescence of carbon quantum dots has not been clarified, the excitation wavelength-dependent emission has been attributed to (a) the presence of CDs of various sizes and (b) the variation of the emission sites formed by the functional groups present on the surface of each nanoparticle [7]. The fluorescence intensity at λemmax increases gradually with increasing excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 340 nm for POS and POS-CYSTE and then decreases. For POS-ETH, there is an increase in the fluorescence intensity at λemmax for λex ranging from 300 nm to 360 nm and then a decrease. The maximum fluorescence intensity is observed at λex = 340 nm for the POS and POS-CYSTE preparations, and at λex = 360 nm for the POS-ETH preparation. After excitation at 340 nm, maximum emission is obtained at 420 nm, 430 nm and 415 nm for POS, POS-ETH and POS-CYSTE, respectively. Consequently, the Stokes shift is 80 nm, 90 nm and 75 nm, for POS, POS-ETH and POS-CYSTE, respectively.

The photostability of the preparation of carbon dots was examined by irradiating (λex = 340 nm) a thermostated (at 30 °C) 20 ug/mL aqueous solution for 360 min and measuring the fluorescence intensity every 0.1 min at λem = 430 nm. Figure S4 shows the remarkable stability of the fluorescence intensity during this time interval.

It has been reported that ethylenediamine (H2NCH2CH2NH2) and cysteamine (H2NCH2CH2SH) increase the quantum yield of CDs from various sources [51–53]. In order to study the effect of ethylenediamine and cysteamine on the quantum yield of the P. oceanica CDs, the CDs were prepared in pure water or in aqueous solutions with various concentrations of ethylenediamine or cysteamine. The quantum yields of the CD preparations were determined by using quinine sulphate as a reference material (Table 3 ). The highest quantum yield of POS-ETH was obtained with 112.5 mmol/L ethylenediamine. The highest quantum yield of POS-CYSTE was obtained with 465 mmol/L cysteamine.

The morphology and the size of the carbon dots were examined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Figure S5 (a) presents TEM images of the CDs. The nanoparticles were nearly spherical with sizes and diameters mainly distributed in the 1–5 nm range. Figure S5 (b) shows the structure obtained by high-resolution TEM (HRTEM). The HRTEM image revealed the crystallinity of the carbon dots with a lattice spacing of 0.331 nm characteristic of the 00.1 direction for graphite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence spectra of carbon dot preparations from POS, POS-ETH and POS-CYSTE was studied in aqueous NaCl solutions in the concentration range of 0 to 1 mol/L. Figures S6, S7 and S8 present graphs of the fluorescence intensity against various NaCl concentrations for CDs prepared from P. oceanica in water (POS) and in aqueous solutions of ethylenediamine (POS-ETH) and cysteamine (POS-CYSTE), respectively. For each CD preparation the fluorescence intensity was measured at the wavelength of the maximum fluorescence intensity. It is observed that the maximum excitation wavelength does not change and the intensity of fluorescence at the wavelength of maximum emission (λemmax) remains constant regardless of the NaCl concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on the fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of pH on the fluorescence spectra was studied over a wide pH range of 2.5 to 12. Citrate buffers (25 mmol/L) were used for the range of pH 2.5 to 6, phosphate buffers (25 mmol/L) were used for pH 7, 8, 11 and 12 and borate buffers (25 mmol/L) were employed for pH 9 and 11. The fluorescence intensity was measured at λex = 340 nm. The pH changes do not cause a shift in λemmax. Data pertaining to the effect of pH on the fluorescence of POS, POS-ETH and POS-CYSTE carbon dots preparations are presented in Fig. 4 . For POS, the fluorescence intensity is practically independent of the pH. For POS-ETH a 46.7% decrease in fluorescence intensity is observed when the pH increases from 2.5 to 12. Finally, for POS-CYSTE, a 35.6% decrease in fluorescence intensity is observed as the pH increases. The dissociation of the carboxyl groups and/or the deprotonation of the amino groups with increasing pH results in the formation of negatively charged nanoparticles with concomitant drop of the fluorescence intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of various ions on the fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of the presence of various inorganic ions, each at a final concentration of 97 µmol/L, on the fluorescence of P. oceanica carbon dot preparations was studied. As ion sources we used the compounds: AgNO3, Al(NO3)3, AsCl3, Ba(NO3)2, Bi(NO3)3, Na2C2O4, Ca(NO3)2, Cd(NO3)2, Ce(NO3)3, Ce(SO4)2, Co(NO3)2, K2Cr2O7, Cr(NO3)3, K2CrO4, Cu(NO3)2, Eu(NO3)3, K4FeCN6, FeSO4, Fe(NO3)3, Hg(NO3)2, Hg2(NO3)2, KI, MgCI2, Mn(NO3)2, Ni(NO3)2, Pb(NO3)2, Na2S, SbCl3, SnCl4, Sr(NO3)2, Zn(NO3)2, Tb(NO3)3. The results for CDs prepared from P. oceanica in water (POS), aqueous ethylenediamine solution (POS-ETH) and aqueous cysteamine solution (POS-CYSTE) are presented in Figures S9, S10 and S11, respectively.

In all three Figures it is observed that most ions do not cause a significant change in the fluorescence intensity. For POS (Figure S9), it is observed that Bi3+ and Hg2+ ions cause a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of about 26% and 47%, respectively. Also, Ce3+ and Ce4+ ions cause a 37% and 65% decrease in fluorescence intensity, respectively. The Cr2O7 2- and CrO4 2- ions cause a decrease of the fluorescence of CDs by 47% and 26%, respectively, which can be attributed to the oxidation of their hydroxyl groups and other surface groups. Finally, Fe3+ ions caused about 70% reduction, which is probably due to the formation of complexes mainly with the hydroxyl groups present on the surface of carbon dots. For POS-ETH (Figure S10), there is an approximately 26% and 23% decrease in fluorescence intensity in the presence of Fe(CN)6 4- and Hg2+ ions, respectively. The Ce3+ and Ce4+ ions cause a 26% and 38% decrease in fluorescence, respectively. The Cr2O7 2-, CrO4 2- and Fe3+ ions also cause a decrease in the fluorescence of CDs by 58%, 40% and 75%, respectively. Finally, for POS-CYSTE (Figure S11), decrease in fluorescence intensity is caused by Cu2+ (29% decrease), Fe(CN)6 4- (37%), Hg2+ (29%), S2- (37%), Ce3+ (34%), Ce4+ (46%), Cr2O7 2- (50%), CrO4 2- (46%) and Fe3+ (59%) ions.

The ATR-IR spectra of the carbon dot preparations in the absence and presence of FeCl3, K2CrO4 and K2Cr2O7 are presented in Figures S12 and S13, respectively. We observe that the ATR-IR spectrum of POS in the presence of Fe3+ is slightly different to that of POS in the absence of Fe3+ in the 1600–500 cm−1 region, due to the Fe3+ coordination which affects the vibrational modes of the nanoparticles. In addition, the spectrum of POS in the presence of Fe3+ exhibits rather strong bands at 3343 and 3198 cm−1 assigned to v(OH) vibrations. This suggests that Fe3+ is bound to hydroxide (OH–) ions derived from the hydrolysis of [Fe(H2O)6]3+.

The ATR-IR spectra of POS in the presence of CrO4 2- and Cr2O7 2- clearly reveal the presence of CrO4 2- and Cr2O7 2- ions. For example, the strong band at 873 cm−1 in the spectrum of POS-CrO4 2- is due to the IR-active v3(F2) [vd (CrO)] vibration. This band is slightly shifted to a lower wavenumber compared with the free CrO4 2- ions (890 cm−1), suggesting a type of interaction (probably through H-bonding) with the nanoparticles. One would expect a splitting of this band because of the simultaneous presence of free and “bound” chromate ions in the sample. Since the two modes are close enough in frequency, the resolution capability of the instrument does not allow the discrimination of these vibrational modes. This is also supported by the relative broad character of this band. Analogous shifts are present in the ATR-IR spectrum of the sample POS-Cr2O4 2-.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric method for determination of Fe3+, CrO4 2- and Cr2O7 2-
ce:section.ce:para: A carbon dot solution of P. oceanica was prepared (POS) in water at a concentration of 13.3 µg/mL. For the determination of Fe3+, a 250 µL volume of Fe3+ solution was added into 3 mL of POS solution and the fluorescence intensity was measured at λex = 340 nm and λem = 430 nm with 5 nm and 10 nm excitation and emission slits, respectively. The fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs at various Fe3+ concentrations, ranging from 0 − 91 μmol/L, are presented in Fig. 5 . A decrease in fluorescence intensity is observed as the concentration of Fe3+ increases. The F0/F ratio was plotted versus the final concentration of Fe3+ in the solution (Fig. 5), where F0 is the fluorescence intensity at zero Fe3+ concentration and F is the fluorescence intensity of the samples. It is observed that Fe3+ can be detected at a concentration of 5 µmol/L. The linear range extends up to 70 µmol/L. The limit of detection (LOD) was 3.2 umol/L.

The quantitative determinations of CrO4 2- and Cr2O7 2- were carried out in a similar way by mixing a 250-μL volume of the solution with 3 mL of POS solution and obtaining the fluorescence spectra as above (see Fig. 6 and S14). Representative calibration graphs for CrO4 2- and Cr2O7 2- are also shown in Fig. 6 and S14. The linear range extends from 5 to 70 µmol/L. The LOD for CrO4 2- and Cr2O7 2- were 7.1 and 12.0 umol/L, respectively.

The fluorescence quenching method described above was applied to the quantitative determination of Fe3+ in a pharmaceutical formulation (Hemafer oral solution, Uni-Pharma, Greece) containing 20 mg/mL Fe3+, in the form of ferric hydroxide polymaltose complex, and, as excipients, sucrose, propyl paraben 216, methyl paraben 218, lemon essence and sorbitol. The analytical procedure involves first the release of Fe3+ from polymaltose complex by acid treatment [54]. For the assay, a 10-uL volume of the sample was diluted to 1 mL with a 0.3 M HCl, 0.08 M HNO3 solution and boiled for 15 min. After cooling down to room temperature, 100 uL of the solution was diluted to 1 mL with 0.04 M NaOH and 300 uL were mixed with 3 mL of 23 ug/mL POS solution in water. The fluorescence was measured at λex = 340 nm and λem = 430 nm. The concentration of Fe3+ in the oral solution was found to be 19.85 mg/mL. The coefficient of variation (CV) was 1.7% (n = 3).

A variety of natural materials have been used as precursors for the preparation of carbon dots with diverse properties and applications. However, in many cases, these materials have high nutritional value, i.e., contain good quality protein, vitamins, minerals and other nutrients. Examples are milk [41], chicken egg [44], tomato juice [42], apple juice [43], peach [38], enokitake mushroom [55]. The novelty of this work is that Posidonia oceanica dead leaves were exploited as a source for fluorescent preparations of carbon dots through hydrothermal processing. The method has low-cost, is environmentally friendly, non-toxic and has potential for scale-up. The CDs were prepared in water as well as in aqueous solution of ethylenediamine and cysteamine and the corresponding fluorescence quantum yields were 3.8%, 11% and 10.6%. The presence of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface offers high water solubility and the potential for preparation of conjugates with several biomolecules. The preparations exhibited excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence emission. The effects of pH, ionic strength and various metal ions on the fluorescence were examined. Finally, it was demonstrated that the fluorescent P. oceanica CD preparations can be used for the determination of Fe3+, Cr2O7 2- and CrO4 2-. The detectabilities for these ions were similar to those offered by carbon dots prepared by other natural or chemical [55,56] materials. Furthermore the applicability of the carbon dots to the determination of Fe3+ in real samples was demonstrated. The carbon dots are not specific for Fe3+. However they are selective. Indeed, from over 30 ions whose effect on the fluorescence of CDs was studied, only a few caused a significant quenching of the fluorescence (e.g. Fe3+, Ce4+, CrO4 2-, Cr2O7 2-). Therefore the CDs can be used as a reagent for the development of fluorometric methods for determination of e.g. Fe3+ in samples in which the other quenching ions are not present in the free ionic form. In conclusion, the present study creates new prospects for the transformation of a widely available waste material to high added-value material for analytical method development and other applications.

Natalia-Maria Christopoulou: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Despina P. Kalogianni: Methodology, Formal analysis, Validation, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition. Theodore K. Christopoulos: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Validation, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge support of this work by the project “Research Infrastructure on Food Bioprocessing Development and Innovation Exploitation – Food Innovation RI” (MIS 5027222), which is implemented under the Action “Reinforcement of the Research and Innovation Infrastructure”, funded by the Operational Programme “Competitiveness, Entrepreneurship and Innovation” (NSRF 2014-2020) and co-financed by Greece and the European Union (European Regional Development Fund). The authors thank Dr. Mary Kollia from the Laboratory of Electron Microscopy and Microanalysis of the University of Patras for her help with the acquisition of TEM images. We are grateful to Prof. Nikolaos Vouroutzis from the Department of Solid State Physics of the Aristotelian University of Thessaloniki for obtaining the HRTEM images. We also thank Dr. Amaia Soto Biobide from the Laboratory of Applied Molecular Spectroscopy of the Institute of Chemical Engineering Sciences (ICE-HT/FORTH) for her help in obtaining the FT-IR spectra. We would like to thank Dr. Labrini Sygellou from ICE-HT/FORTH for the XPS measurements and analysis. We are also thankful to Prof. Spyridon Perlepes from the Department of Chemistry, University of Patras, for his valuable comments on ATR spectra.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105787.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
